speaker: interviewer
text: E-E-F-I-R-E-S-D-O-N-E. I'm speaking to Max Stodel. It's March 14, 1995. And we're speaking in English. We're talking about Westerbork, and how you were digging trenches to build a morgue.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that mean to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, if you're 19 years of age, I think about the time 19, because it was 1942. I was 19 years of age. And if you realize now that I'm 71, what I did, what I had to do-- what they made me do-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you can understand I get emotional.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the morgue for?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were only Jews there. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So you can imagine who it was for.

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of Jews died in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a paper here. And I looked to it last night. And I'm going to show you that later, if you're interested, that to my surprise-- really, one or two-- on a partial list, a very small partial-- very, very small part-- that two died in Westerbork. And I was stunned last night, because now they came-- all ages, they came there. I got admitted because my family was from-- from one year old to my father, the oldest-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 61 years old at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So only two people died in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a very, very small list here, very small-- only from the two-- two families' name-- my wife's, second wife's, and my own. And maybe later on, it is interesting to pick that up there, because I got it down on a-- on a printed piece of paper. From-- I don't-- my wife says my daughter got it in [NON-ENGLISH], but she might have gotten it in Holland. I don't remember where she got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what was life like in Westerbork.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bragging-- everybody can brag, but nobody-- I mean, so many who brag who can back it up, but I can back it up. The word, [GERMAN]-- you might know in German, you don't have to go. The-- I don't know the English translation. You don't have to go because you're-- you're-- I can't translate it. Anyway, to make a long story short, on Friday night, they picked some people who didn't have to go to Germany on account they needed the good workers in Westerbork. I was the second one-- one above me-- I have to admit it. He got some identification that he didn't have to go to the camp. I was the second. Sure enough, that Sunday [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] my father and wife came to Westerbork. And, like I said, I was spared to go to Germany, or sent to wherever they send me to. And that Sunday morning, when my father and my wife came, the next day they want to send them away-- away from Holland to Germany, or Poland-- wherever they send them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went up to the Germans there. And I told them-- no, I was-- I said to my father, and my wife, and my aunt, what I told you before, if you go, I go. So we're sitting in the train, and I said to whoever it was, I was spared-- the Friday night. So I am not supposed to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I want my father, my aunt-- but I called her my mother at the time, because my mother died. That was the story about my aunt, even though she looks like my father, and my wife-- I was taken off the train with my wife, with my father, and my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this officer who took you off?

speaker: interviewee
text: You've got me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't know who intervened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I had an ID. I-- I was not supposed to go. But my main goal was not for me alone. I got my father off-- I got my father, my wife, and my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you all stay in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there for another two, three weeks, when I got word that my father and my aunt-- my mother-- had to go the next day. And, naturally, I did it again. I said, I'm not-- I'm going to go with you. I talked to the Germans again. But I never heard it before, a case like this. Sure enough, they told me, I didn't report that I was off the train to whoever. I didn't know I had to. That's why they send me. Maybe because the work that I did was already done, so they didn't need me anymore-- because you couldn't trust them. This is proof what kind of Germans they are.

speaker: interviewer
text: So four of us went on the train. We left November 16, 1942. And we were on the train, I think, for two nights. And we arrived in Kozel. Kozel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we arrived in somewhere. Anyway, they took-- then we had the separation, all the people one way, younger people the other way. I was carrying my father's bag. He was carrying my suitcase.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father was sent the one way. I never saw him again. I was carrying my suitcase, what I had, what-- much larger clothes than mine. So-- but anyway, they was taken away, anyway, like they're-- and all that kind of stuff.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was November 19, what I got on paper from the official.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go? Where did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Annaberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife was sent-- all the people one day, and the wives and the men were also separate-- the younger was also separate. So my-- she went with my father's side, where the older were. And I went to the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife went on my father's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the older people?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the older people, the women and the older-- the older and the women on one side, and the young and strong-- they need slave--

speaker: interviewer
text: But all the women went with the old people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were they sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've never-- I can only say what I heard later. And they were sent to Auschwitz. I didn't see that-- I can't say that they did go to Auschwitz. But I see on paper that my father died November 19, 1942. My wife died November, 1942. My aunt died November, 1942. They must have all gone to Auschwitz. They died the same day.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know they died in November?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have it on paper here.

speaker: interviewer
text: From where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to show you later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just tell me where you got the paper, from whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you, before, either from [NON-ENGLISH], or from the Dutch government, that I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were records of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you don't know where?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter got it from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but you don't know where they died. You just know that they died.

speaker: interviewee
text: The paper says Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK, that's what I'm asking. So they all went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: They all went to Auschwitz, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, how about your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother went to the camp-- was sent away from Westerbork, because I spot both-- no, no, no. My youngest brother, I saw in Westerbork. And he was sent away to Germany, Poland, whatever they did. That's my brother--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other brother-- also on paper I got that. My older brother, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he was sent away in '43, September '43. And he--

speaker: interviewer
text: From Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Westerbork, or from-- yeah, I don't know if he was in Westerbork or not, because I was already in Germany. But like I said, I got a very, very partial list, very small partial list, from the Jews from the Netherlands who died-- only the-- my name and my second wife's names. And he is one-- he is one of the two people who only said he died in Poland, not in Sobibor, or or Auschwitz, or Blechhammer, or wherever. There are two on that list. And my brother is one of them who died in Poland, it says. There are also on that list people who are from [NON-ENGLISH], you know, who are the-- I think now that I think that I-- I think the word for [NON-ENGLISH] is Dutch. She must have gotten the paper in the Netherlands.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sisters? My oldest sister was sent away while I was in Westerbork-- I saw her in Westerbork. My second oldest sister, I saw in Westerbork. My other sister-- one of the two sister I didn't see. And neither did I see my fourth sister, only the two oldest and my youngest brother in Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: None of the sisters and brothers came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: None of them came back, only the youngest child of my youngest sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got married? But you never lived with your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never lived with my wife because I got married one day. I got sent away the next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after everybody was taken away and you remained in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was taken away? You mean, who, from my family?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: From my family? I had to work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the day that I was sent away.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was sent away? Finally, I was sent away November 16, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Annaburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Annaburg, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Annaburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to mislead you. But my memory, 1951 I got a paper here but it's in Dutch, from the government. At that time, I stated the dates and how long I was there. And if you give me a chance to look up the paper, I can tell you exactly--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just tell me approximately what year.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was maybe 10 days or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from there you went where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Klein Mangersdorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same length of time, maybe seven, eight, 10 days. I don't know exactly. I got it on paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from here?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Klein Mangersdorf, we went to Blechhammer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And stayed in Blechhammer how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: From December 4, 1942 to January 21, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: From December 4, '42?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 21st of January, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you liberated here? Were you liberated in Blechhammer?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no-- the trouble started when we left January the 21st. We walked for 14 days up to Gross-Rosen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you here, you were in Blechhammer for three years?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not three years, two years and a couple of months.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what were you doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Work. I was a foreman again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Foreman doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Doing what, very good question. It was more than two years. But let me tell you something. Before I bragged-- or you can call it bragging-- that I was always a good worker, I was even such a good worker that is unbelievable to people who were in the camp who can only imagine what it was. The maestra, they called it, he gave me his bicycle to do the chores, what I had to do, and what I did do. It's indescribable. It takes maybe 20 videos if I tell you what I did, because I'm an active person, what I did. Even so much, that if the German knows what I did to sabotage them, they might start a war just to get a hold of me, and to kill me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, give us a little idea what you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I delivered the goods. What the maestra want, the boss wants, he got delivered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Delivered to whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I did, I did transportation. I had to pick, for instance, a pipe here, bring it over there. It had to be sawed. Then I had the other pipe that's connecting. And all-- they build it-- they build it to make synthetic material for the war. And he was so taken in with me that in wintertime, we couldn't do as much work because in Poland this can be pretty cold. That he, I can't say it in English-- maybe, it explains it better in German. He protected us in wintertime, only to keep the same people to do work for him again when summer came-- that he even sabotaged the German soldiers. He put us away in a corner that nobody would see us doing nothing all day long in wintertime, only to have us back. If he got better paid that we did good work, and all that kind of work, because we were not interested in working for them. But since I did it to his satisfaction. One day, the wagon broke down. I had to break up pipes. And we were skin and bones, all of us. So the wagon broke down. And we didn't deliver the goods that night, because they worked 24 hours there in that-- I got the name here, [INAUDIBLE] working in Blechhammer. And so the next morning, he said, I will get you for that. You didn't-- at me-- you didn't do what you're supposed to do. So we worked two weeks before we get one Sunday off. We work Saturday and Sunday. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And so instead of giving us a wagon, he gave us a heavy pipe to carry on our shoulders, the small fore, and the back ones in the back. I was the boss of that group, let's say seven, eight, nine people, whatever it was, how many you need. And he told us, you don't get a wagon today, on a Sunday, on a Sunday. You've got to carry it on your shoulders. We were skin and bones.

speaker: interviewer
text: So let's say I walked in the middle, because I always worked with the people, even though I was not-- I couldn't-- the boss saw us carrying on our shoulders the pipe. He pulled me, he pulled me out under the pipe. I didn't have to carry. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: When we walked for two week, when I told you before January 21, 1945, we just walked by where he lived. We walked for days-- I don't know if you worked because you were in a camp, also. They were on the street making fun of us that we walked with skins and bones, no shoes, no clothes, no decent sleeping. We slept at night in haystacks. He brought out bread for me out of his house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Listen, I was able-- if I was able to write, I cannot write one book. I can write 50 books, because you experience something 24 hours a day for so many years as I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: But is there anything that you want us to know?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I did?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of work I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: About Blechhammer, about Blechhammer.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if you've got enough film in there. But I can tell about Blechhammer. And I got to back it up. When I worked one day in Blechhammer where they did the synthetic work, I heard two people speaking Dutch to each other. We were not to speak. We were not supposed to speak with Gentiles. I walked behind them. And I spoke with him. We were not facing each other. And I asked them if they can do something for me. I was desperate. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't know if they were kosher, those guys, if they turn on me because I spoke to them. I took a chance, I was very desperate, hunger. They brought me a paper, because they worked in Germany also. They was both sent from the government-- Dutch government, German government, whatever it was in Germany, to work certain amount of time in Germany for the war. They brought me a Dutch paper. One thing rolled into the other. They went [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on holiday for a week, two weeks, whatever it was. It's immaterial.

speaker: interviewer
text: I ask them, since I had my first wife's sister living in Holland on account she was married to a Gentile, if you want to take a letter, if you want to take something with me to give my sister-in-law? Yes, he said. I will do that for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took a chance. I could have been killed. I did write a letter, not on stationery-- I teared off a piece of cement sack where cement was in. Don't ask me where I got the pen or the pencil. Ballpoint does not exist at the time. I really don't know how I got the pen, if he gave it to me or whatever. And he took for me a letter to my sister-in-law who was living on a certain address at the time. That's a whole story in itself. And he was not from this same city. He went out of his way. He went to my sister-in-law. My brother-in-law, who was a Gentile, worked in the underground-- very risky, me not knowing that he worked in the underground. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I'm going to just put in-- I brought my-- my brother-in-law, the Gentile brother, I brought him-- I brought him over to the United States on vacation, on my expense. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever meet this Gentile Dutch man who took the letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was more than one who did it. And if I-- if you have time all day, I would like to bring it across from what I-- what I did. So it was very risky, like I said.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he brought back-- my sister-in-law gave him 100 mark at the time, 100 guilder at the time. He brought me back every penny of it-- 131 mark and 20 pennies for 100 guilder at the time. What am I going to do with money in concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I work at the [NON-ENGLISH]. And I work, you name it, Ukrainians, Russian, French, New Zealanders, Australia's, you name it. Everybody works there. The Australians, and the English, and the French that were prisoners of war. And even a name on that list what I got here, and men worked for union labor in the Netherlands. And one of the soldiers, one of the PO-- POW from England, I think-- or from Australia-- I'm-- I'm not sure. They got to talk to each other. And that man, that soldier, saw to it that he got all the bread from the guy's leftover. And he got it because they worked for the same company at the time. His name, I got him here, [NON-ENGLISH] was his name. I wander off a little bit, I know. But [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I forgot where I was, because I wandered off.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about the fact that you was working with all these nationalities together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK, that guy who who put the-- took the cement sack with me, brought me everything back, all the money back. So the next time when he went to the Netherlands again-- maybe two, three, four months later. I don't know what it was. And maybe it was not him. And maybe it was another man that they did some-- that was more than one who was willing to do it. He brought me back food from my sister-in-law. My sister-in-law gave him food. He got a ginger cake that was so long that he had to give it in half.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how could he hand it to me? There was no way he could hand it to me. We went into the men's room. And he put it down. And I picked it up. Where did I hide my money? At that time, I had a pair of pants. And I cut it open a little bit. And I stuck it not in my pocket, but in a belt line.

speaker: interviewee
text: And just to mention that one day, sure enough, we were stopped. And the Germans saw me throwing the money away. Oh, I got it in the face. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't use the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used-- not all the money I throwed away, because-- I never forget, it was three mark for a half loaf of bread, and six mark for a whole loaf of bread. But I bought little bit at a time. What was I also able to buy? Yes, unbelievable what you hear here, because what was I also able to buy? Like Crisco-- Crisco you know? I need fat for your body, winter time. Later on, I found out it was dog fumph-- it was fat from a dog what I ate. I was raised kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you buy the stuff from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody I could, because everybody was selling bread, because some people-- not from the Jews, not from Jews, from the people who work in the [NON-ENGLISH]. From the Ukrainians I bought it. I bought it from-- the French always gave it away, and the English and the Australia, because I never forget, one day I ask them to warm up some potato that we stole. And he said, OK, it's going to be there, laying there, 12:30, let's say. And not only were the potatoes fried for us, for the men I worked with-- I didn't look only after myself. I looked out for more people. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: He made a nice little package with all kinds of remnants of bread, you know? And he put down cigarettes only for people-- because we had people with us who smoke, who had a smoke an [INAUDIBLE]. So you worked together with the non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't work together with them. I didn't work together. I met them at the [NON-ENGLISH]. They worked there. We worked there. But we worked as a group. And they might have worked with individuals or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like in the place where you lived in Blechhammer? Was it in barracks, in houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barracks, barracks-- now, I want to come back to, if I may--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: To that-- to that Gentiles who did-- sent to get to Holland. One day, one guy went back to Holland-- went back to Holland. And I don't know where he was from, but said he was from. And my sister moved-- my sister-in-law moved from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you asked me before how was it living in Holland between the Gentiles and the Jews? I happened to know that guy, an Aryan and Gentile guy. He's--
